title: NYU-Abu-Dhabi iGEM 2025 Results Figures and Tables
author: Pandit, Udaya
license_note: Zenodo record metadata license field: cc-by-4.0
description:
The NYU-Abu-Dhabi iGEM 2025 team developed Revitalyze, a project that aims to develop therapeutics for congenital disorders of glycosylation (CDGs), a group of rare metabolic diseases caused by defects in various stages of the glycosylation pathway. Errors in glycosylation can confuse cellular interactions, causing serious malfunctions in multiple organs and organ systems. The most common type of CDG is PMM2-CDG (or CDG-Ia), where the mutation in the PMM2 enzyme, which converts mannose-6-phosphate to mannose-1-phosphate, leads to decreased glycosylation rate. As a comprehensive solution to this problem, Revitalyze proposes engineering probiotics that deliver the functional PMM2 protein to the patient’s body cells via bacterial extracellular vesicles (bEVs), thereby allowing an enzyme replacement therapy that minimizes disruption in the patients' daily lives. Starting with PMM2-CDG, our delivery system can be expanded to any other type of CDG caused by mutations in enzymes, and potentially other disorders as well. 
 In doing so, we developed a genetic toolkit to engineer  E. coli  to produce customized, protein-loaded extracellular vesicles (EVs). To accomplish this, we begin with the construction of our genetic parts library, followed by assembling parts using Golden Gate Assembly. We then validate our design by confirming protein expression with a fluorescent reporter. Next, we establish the downstream workflow, from the optimization of the EV isolation protocol to the characterization of the isolated EVs by cryo-TEM. Finally, to explore the real-world potential of Revitalyze, we present a proof-of-concept study on the viability of our chassis,  E. coli  Nissle 1917, in milk. 
 The files included are figures and tables representing our results, which we explain in more detail on our  Wiki  and  relevant parts in our Part Collection . 
   
  Figure and Table descriptions:  
 Table 1. Cloning status of Level 0 parts obtained from the iGEM distribution kit. 
 Table 2.  Status of Level 1 Golden Gate Assemblies for PMM2 and mCherry constructs. 
 Figure 1. Correctly assembled plasmids result in non-fluorescent colonies 
 Figure 2. The  E. coli  DH5α plates, viewed under blue light, show minimal visible fluorescence. From left to right, the constructs are OmpA-(GS)₃-GFLG-(GS)₄-mCherry, OmpA-GGGGS-mCherry, ssTorA-mCherry, and OmpA-EAAAK-mCherry. 
 Figure 3. The image shows the fluorescence of different constructs in  E. coli  DH5α under a fluorescence microscope TRITC filter with 40x magnification: OmpA-GGGGS-mCherry (top left, highest intensity), OmpA-(GS)₃GFLG(GS)₄-mCherry (top right), OmpA-EAAAK-mCherry (bottom left), and ssTorA-mCherry (bottom right). 
 Figure 4. Transformation of  E. coli  BL21. These plates show colonies of  E. coli  BL21 transformed with various mCherry expression constructs. 
 Figure 5. Growth curve of  E. coli  Nissle 1917 (EcN). The optical density at 600 nm (OD600) was measured over 30 hours to determine that the optimal harvest time for cells in the late exponential phase is approximately 9 hours 
 Figure 6. Schematic of the Extracellular Vesicle (EV) isolation protocol. This timeline illustrates the optimized three-day workflow, which includes inoculation, cell growth, two centrifugation steps, filtration, two ultracentrifugation steps, and preparation for analysis. Created in  https://BioRender.com  
 Figure 7. EV pellet from wild-type  E. coli  Nissle 1917 (EcN). A visible pellet of isolated EVs is shown after ultracentrifugation of the wild-type, non-engineered strain. 
 Figure 8. EV pellet from  E. coli  BL21 expressing OmpA-GGGGS-mCherry. A visible pellet containing isolated EVs is shown post-ultracentrifugation. 
 Figure 9. EV pellet from  E. coli  BL21 expressing OmpA-EAAAK-mCherry. A visible pellet containing isolated EVs is shown post-ultracentrifugation. 
 Figure 10. EV pellet from  E. coli  BL21 expressing OmpA-(GS)₃GFLG(GS)₄-mCherry.  A visible pellet containing isolated EVs is shown post-ultracentrifugation. 
 Figure 11. EV isolation attempt from  E. coli  BL21 expressing ssTorA-mCherry.
